steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the Algeria information within the text as a whole. Identify what Algeria does for the argument: it presents a real-world fuel shift, which the text uses to set up a critique of the energy ladder model.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It provides an example of a type of change that the text goes on to suggest is poorly suited for evaluating whether the energy ladder is a viable model.: The Algeria example is not presented as poorly suited for evaluation—it is the exact type of phenomenon the energy ladder is meant to explain. The text uses it to show the model is applied, not to question whether it can be evaluated. ✗
  • Choice B — It introduces a finding that the text goes on to suggest can be explained in two different ways that are equally compelling.: The text does not present two equally compelling explanations. It presents the energy ladder explanation and then Heltberg's finding that this explanation is reductive—one view is critiqued, not balanced against another. ✗
  • Choice C — It describes a trend that the text goes on to suggest has a similar cause as a seemingly unrelated trend observed in Ghana.: The text does not say Algeria and Ghana share a cause. Ghana is where Heltberg studied fuel use to critique the energy ladder; it is not a parallel trend with Algeria. ✗
  • Choice D — It illustrates the kind of phenomenon that the text goes on to suggest is frequently but inadequately accounted for by the energy ladder.: The text says fuel shifts like Algeria's are ‘often explained’ by the energy ladder, then uses Heltberg to show that explanation is ‘reductive’—i.e., inadequate. Algeria illustrates the kind of phenomenon the model frequently but incompletely explains. ✓
That matches Choice D — It illustrates the kind of phenomenon that the text goes on to suggest is frequently but inadequately accounted for by the energy ladder..
